title: Interview With the Vampire Changes Everything in Lestat’s S3

blocks.content: https://pyxis.nymag.com/v1/imgs/b05/809/65eaa5af8986100cbe27d8f4e72992b504-iwtv-1.rhorizontal.w1100.jpg
This article originally ran on June 2, 2026. The Vampire Lestat premieres on AMC and AMC+ on June 7.

Inside Toronto’s historic Danforth Music Hall, on a stage littered with ornate rugs and illuminated by dozens of overlapping fuchsia, tangerine, and lavender lights, a vampire is singing a diss track. Actor Sam Reid is laced into a pair of skintight black pants, his shoulder-length blond hair braided into Britney-style pigtails and his face and torso covered in iridescent glitter. Nearly 300 extras are screaming in front of him, clutching red Solo cups and dressed to match their idol: pleather, fishnets, chunky boots. Reid prowls the length of the stage before bursting into a high-energy song-and-dance number. Every few takes, a crew member sprays him down with a mixture of water and fake blood, careful not to damage his custom Fender Stratocaster, which is already spattered with its own red mess. A regular hosing-down is “quite refreshing, actually,” Reid tells me later.

For hours now, Reid and the four actors who make up his backing band have been performing one of the 20-plus original songs written for the new season of AMC’s Interview With the Vampire, now renamed The Vampire Lestat. This song is “Big Boss,” a “Hey Mickey”–style takedown of Lestat de Lioncourt’s many enemies, including his ex-lover Louis de Pointe du Lac (Jacob Anderson), who exposed the existence of vampires (and the lurid details of his relationship with Lestat) to all of humanity in a best-selling book. Lestat, now a contemporary rock star who writes songs about living openly undead, demonstrates his contempt for Interview With the Vampire by jeering at audience members who might have read it. Reid waves a paperback around before tossing it behind his back. (In one take, he accidentally hits a camera operator and apologizes.) Lestat then turns the insults on himself: “I am actually the toxic bitch, anxiously attached show pony, with a personality disorder,” Reid screams while doing a horselike gallop. For the scene’s wide shot, he jumps onto the venue floor and moves through a tunnel of extras to sing directly in the face of another nemesis: Armand (Assad Zaman), the ancient vampire who wooed his ex and has been standing in the crowd, glaring, this whole time. Reid writhes in front of Zaman, sticking out his tongue in one take, running his hand down his body in another, all while a gigantic technocrane zooms overhead. When the scene cuts, Reid and Zaman boogie around each other, Pulp Fiction style.

This concert — and the others dotted throughout the seven-episode season as part of Lestat’s cross-country tour — are some of the loudest, most flamboyant scenes staged by a series beloved for being louder and more flamboyant than most things on television. Its first two seasons were unlike anything else, an audacious gothic romance that renders explicit the homoerotic subtext of Anne Rice’s first novel. Series creator and showrunner Rolin Jones stuck close to the tumultuous, decades-spanning relationship between vampires Louis and Lestat that was familiar to readers, but he made two of the three main characters Black and many of the central relationships obviously queer. Years before Heated Rivalry centered two hot men consumed by a secret romance, IWTV had Louis and Lestat nude and levitating while making love, arguing about their feelings and their future. (Louis and Lestat would each blame the other for being problematic in their romance; both of them would be right.) “If ‘Yes, Shane Hollander, but a shower of blood’ sounds good to you, then watch The Vampire Lestat,” says executive producer and writer Hannah Moscovitch, who is friends with Heated Rivalry creator and fellow Canadian Jacob Tierney. “Theirs is beautiful. Ours is fucking dark-sided.”

IWTV’s first two seasons are framed by the infamous interview Louis gives to journalist and Interview With the Vampire writer Daniel Molloy (Eric Bogosian) and adorned with lots of operatic set pieces, gallons of viscera, dialogue that prances between lustful and hateful, and Anderson and Reid’s magnetic chemistry. The baroque melodrama of Rice’s prose remained intact, but the episodes tackled America and Europe’s penchants for racism, xenophobia, and homophobia more directly. The second season culminated in a play staged by the bloodsucking troupe Théâtre des Vampires in which Louis and Lestat’s vampire daughter, Claudia (Delainey Hayles), is killed onstage before a cheering audience of humans who can’t quite believe what they’re seeing, a stunning encapsulation of the show’s high and low taste. AMC is “the first network ever that I could say, ‘The thing that people see on television is the thing I wanted to make,’” says Jones, a Pulitzer-finalist playwright who put in time on Friday Night Lights, Weeds, and Boardwalk Empire.

And now, in its third season, the show is blowing itself up. It’s changed its name to acknowledge its new source material (Rice’s second novel) and to signal Lestat’s newfound, fourth-wall-breaking control of the narrative. It is now structured around his band’s tour, for which Reid had to record original songs and perform them on set. The season starts with its central couple in the throes of a breakup, much to the chagrin of the series’ most fervent fans, who call the pairing “Loustat,” describe them as “true soulmates” who “match each other’s freak … in the most unconventional manner,” and soundtrack TikTok odes to their relationship with music from Megan Thee Stallion and Sufjan Stevens. (Molloy calling Louis and Lestat “Taylor and Travis” in a season-three clip sent reverberations through the base.) Will Vampire’s loyal, if still modest, fandom buy into an experiment that changes the show’s primary protagonist and its established storytelling mode, complete with an incest storyline that puts George R.R. Martin’s bibliography to shame? More important, can it rally the kind of viewership and mainstream awareness that turned Heated Rivalry into a phenomenon and guarantee a future past season three?

“They want to be punched in the nose all the time,” Jones says of the series’ devoted audience. “I think they want that visceral feeling. Maybe they’ll think out of the gate, Oh, this isn’t as depthful or This seems like they’re scared. But we made a simple promise: Don’t expect the first two seasons. And there’s always a design, y’all. You’ve just got to wait.”

When Rice published Interview With the Vampire in 1976, some reviews dismissed it as not scary enough, calling it “too superficial, too impersonal and too obviously made.” (Years later, in a 1995 piece for The New York Review of Books, Joyce Carol Oates disagreed, calling Rice’s oeuvre “lushly overdone” sagas of “erotic horror.”) Rice’s Louis de Pointe du Lac was a slave owner on a Louisiana plantation at the end of the 18th century who gets turned into a vampire by an “extraordinary” and braggadocious undead Frenchman, Lestat de Lioncourt. Louis narrates the exploits of his found family: how he and a reluctant Lestat transform a young girl, Claudia, into a vampire; how Claudia and Louis try to kill the imperious and controlling Lestat; how Louis and Claudia travel to Europe and meet Armand, the leader of the Théâtre des Vampires troupe and coven. Louis’s memories form an immersive, spellbinding confession of betrayal, murder, and melancholy with palpable sensuality. Rice describes Louis feeding on a willing human while feeling the “hard strength of his sex” and almost immediately afterward meeting Armand and experiencing “the wakening of a need so terrible that the very promise of its fulfillment contained the unbearable possibility of disappointment.”

Before the hardcover came out, publishers engaged in a bidding war for paperback rights and Paramount Pictures showed interest in an adaptation. When it finally published, the book wasn’t an immediate commercial success, but it had already started a slow and steady burn into cult-classic status. Rice wrote 12 sequels over 42 years (selling 150 million copies in total) and inspired two film adaptations: 1994’s Interview With the Vampire, which featured Brad Pitt and Tom Cruise as Louis and Lestat, and 2002’s Queen of the Damned, released after the 2001 death of co-lead and R&B star Aaliyah. Each movie had its charms (Cruise is wonderfully chaotic-evil as Lestat, even if Rice initially said he was “no more my vampire Lestat than Edward G. Robinson is Rhett Butler”; she eventually came around), but neither adaptation committed to the queerness, or violence, of Rice’s oeuvre.
https://pyxis.nymag.com/v1/imgs/040/1b9/d7160cef5c6733e4dfd09a466da041850b-tomcruise-bradpitt.rhorizontal.w700.jpg

By the late 2010s, vampires were a culturally omnipresent, money-making genre force: There was Blade (1998), Buffy the Vampire Slayer (1997–2003), Twilight (2008), and True Blood (2008–14). In December 2019, Variety reported that the rights to Rice’s 18 novels across the Vampire Chronicles and Lives of the Mayfair Witches series were up for sale for somewhere between $40 million and $50 million. In May 2020, AMC struck a deal for an undisclosed amount of money, gaining control of both properties and planning an interconnected suite of shows under the Immortal Universe title. “It felt like a great companion piece to the audience that was showing up to our platforms for Walking Dead,” says Ben Davis, executive vice-president of programming for AMC Studios. He considers Lestat and Louis “lightning-in-a-bottle characters” alongside Mad Men’s Don Draper, Breaking Bad’s Walter White, and Better Call Saul’s Saul Goodman.

In 2014, Rolin Jones wrote a pilot for AMC called Knifeman, a historical drama about a graverobbing surgeon starring Tom Hollander and future Vampire Lestat cast member Jennifer Ehle. When it failed to go to series, Jones moved to HBO to reboot Perry Mason, staying through that show’s first season before entering into a newly brokered overall deal at AMC in May 2020. During his first meeting with AMC execs, someone mentioned Interview With the Vampire. “Don’t give that job to anybody yet,” Jones remembers saying. “Let me see what I want to do.” He watched the 1994 adaptation, read the first novel and its three initial sequels (1985’s The Vampire Lestat, 1988’s The Queen of the Damned, and 1992’s The Tale of the Body Thief), and realized this could fulfill his desire to write a grown-up romance. “In terms of the love-story part, the book it reminds me of the most is Wuthering Heights,” he says. “There are really crazy, over-the-top feels in it. Anne’s great seduction, the thing she gave to the vampire genre, was ‘You are the vampire.’” He returned to AMC a few weeks later with a 12-page document outlining his vision to adapt Rice’s first four books into four seasons of television. In his saga, Louis is a Black brothel owner in New Orleans in the 1920s who loves jazz and is seduced by Lestat after watching him feed on the priest to whom Louis was confessing his sins. “Be my companion, Louis. Be all the beautiful things you are, and be them without apology, for all eternity,” Lestat coos, and they make out under a statue of Jesus Christ on the cross. It’s “not naturalism,” Moscovitch says. “It’s stylized as fuck. We’re going to give people poetry to say.”

AMC announced a one-season order for Interview With the Vampire in June 2021, but Jones quickly realized he’d need two seasons to adapt all of the first book and build out the loyalties and enmities between its five primary characters. There was some back-and-forth with AMC about when the series should emphasize violence or amp up the romance. (“‘Where’s the genre shit?’” as Jones describes one early network note.) But Davis says AMC ultimately had faith in Jones’s ability to “contemporize” Rice’s work, delivering new material for fans who thought they knew everything about her story and newbies tuning in with no knowledge of the books. Crucial events morphed into massive set pieces, like Louis hunting down Claudia’s killers by setting their theater on fire, exploding the motorcycles of the vampires who fled the inferno, and eventually bursting out of a manhole to behead Claudia’s main tormenter. Some epic scenes were invented wholesale, like a tearful reunion between Louis and Lestat in their old New Orleans home as a hurricane rages around them. (Both those sequences took place in the season-two finale “And That’s the End of It. There’s Nothing Else.,” which at that point was the series’ most ambitious episode.) There was quite a lot of fucking too, sometimes rough, sometimes tender, between the main characters. But AMC limits onscreen nudity, so much of the carnality is communicated in dialogue — Lestat suggesting to Louis that they have an orgy, so “you can fuck them and I can eat them,” or Louis declaratively telling Armand to take his clothes off and get “face down in the coffin.” IWTV could be as narratively ambitious, and visually stunning, as it could be explicit. “Everyone should be very grateful for that, especially hard-core fans who would like some more hot sex on our show than we’ve given them,” Jones says.
https://pyxis.nymag.com/v1/imgs/c49/343/16b3d57b5afcf65e39403644006c4f47e5-Interview-With-the-Vampire.rhorizontal.w700.jpg

And Jones is, for the record, deeply appreciative of those fans. When a production assistant created a five-panel collage shaped like the IWTV logo and composed of hundreds of pieces of fan art, he featured it in his season-three writers’ room in Los Angeles, then shipped it to Toronto when the series moved there for filming. Those fans packed the IWTV panels at San Diego Comic-Con in both 2024 and 2025 and shared surreptitiously recorded clips of the series’s trailers and performances of season two’s original songs by composer Daniel Hart. “It’s an astonishing experience for us all to be a part of something where people feel like they would go to war on behalf of these fictional characters,” Reid says. Representatives for AMC declined to provide specific viewership numbers for IWTV, describing it obscurely as “a top series on AMC+,” the channel’s streaming service. “From the time it premiered in May of 2024, Interview With the Vampire season two was the No. 2 season — across all shows — in both viewership and acquisition for a full year, only behind The Walking Dead: The Ones Who Live.” However, compared with a behemoth like the original Walking Dead, IWTV’s numbers seem low: Linear viewership counts at IWTV’s highest, according to Nielsen, were a fraction of The Walking Dead at its lowest. But Jones says he’s been told “there is quite high retention — people who watch it then watch it again and again.”

“We are certainly under no illusion that we are making a show for everybody,” Jones says. “But I think we have made something that feels like, for a lot of people, their favorite show on TV right now. And for a smaller group of that, their favorite show that’s ever been on television. I don’t know if it’s the largest tent in the world, but it’s a weird and diverse tent.”

Most of The Vampire Lestat’s production is tucked inside a suburban Toronto strip mall with a Firehouse Subs down the street. (Previous Vampire seasons filmed in New Orleans, Prague, and Paris, but season three took shape in Toronto across four studios and various real concert venues subbing in for cities like Detroit and Indianapolis.) The day after the “Big Boss” scene, co-producer Maria Jungvirt is showing me around the sprawling complex. Carpenters are busy dismantling sets and repurposing them for the next block of episodes. Inside the one that serves as Lestat’s luxe tour bus, someone is sanding down his wooden coffin. There’s spilled cereal, guitar picks, empty liquor bottles, and a “First groupie of the tour” condom nailed to a wall. Elsewhere, a set dressed like the streets of 18th-century Paris for flashback scenes has hay and dirt scattered everywhere, stale bread and limp vegetables in wooden carts, and French Revolution slogans painted on the walls. On yet another stage, a set acting as a Detroit basement is decorated with bathtubs and grimy plastic curtains made to look like the lair of a vampire group called the Fang Gang. One crew member passes us carrying a goopy-looking decapitated head in a plastic bag, another with a serrated-edge sword. In the scene they’re about to start filming, Louis is “going to remove someone’s spine,” producer Adam O’Byrne whispers to me. “The action in the script was something like, ‘He drops the vertebrae like dice.’”

The series’ cast and crew considered the third season so different from what they’d made before that they started referring to the first episode as a pilot rather than a premiere. Moscovitch describes the first 15 episodes of IWTV as “moody existentialism” told from Louis’s point of view. Now it’s Lestat’s past and perspective — that of a “toxic blond, a chaotic princess,” she says — shaping the series, as it does the book. But while Rice’s second novel is set in the 1980s, Jones’s adaptation of it takes place in 2025. And while Louis and Molloy are mostly absent from the source material, Jones and the writers’ room invented new story lines for them, sometimes repurposing plot points from future Vampire Chronicles entries. (Molloy, newly turned into a vampire by Armand, is still doing the interviewing, now via a documentary about Lestat’s tour.) Two new characters arrive in season three: Lestat’s mother, Gabriella, and the first vampire, Akasha — proud, self-assured “vampires who are like, ‘This is for me,’” Moscovitch says — played by Ehle and Sheila Atim, respectively. Lestat’s relationship with his family, during both their human lives and their vampire lives, is complicated. A flashback dinner scene at the Lioncourt estate where Lestat stabs one of his terribly conceited brothers and Gabriella barely looks up from the book she’s reading conveys the general dysfunction. Ehle calls the Lestat-Gabriella dynamic “abusive.” Having seen the season, I would use another adjective, but that would spoil the horror to come.
https://pyxis.nymag.com/v1/imgs/e5c/e3a/0fdce8d6286c026c7009ab20aeb1b6ba7a-iwtv-3.rhorizontal.w700.jpg

Despite the divergences, Jones says the writers’ room’s guiding light for the season was a moment from season one’s “Like Angels Put in Hell by God” when Louis swims across the Mississippi River to confront Lestat about recording a song inspired by Louis, but with Lestat’s female lover (who Louis hates) singing on the track. Louis appears soaking wet in Lestat’s bedroom with the vinyl in his hand, surprising Lestat and Antoinette (Maura Grace Athari) while they’re nude in bed. Lestat, gleeful that Louis has returned to him, orders Antoinette out of the room, then Louis and Lestat fight and then (of course) have sex. “That was, for all of us, the most batshit crazy we ever got. And it was like, ‘That has to be the season,’” Jones says. “That’s what we were aiming for over and over again.” The writers’ room took six months, from fall 2024 to spring 2025. Jones says Reid “famously” threw his laptop when he read a certain scene in third episode “Toronto,” took a walk around the building, then came back in, finished reading the script, and had a 180-degree reaction, ultimately laughing at his prior irritation when he realized where the episode ended up. “There was a lot of panic everywhere from the first time the first pages started coming in,” Jones says. “Everybody’s like, ‘What is this? What the hell are you doing?’ We really wrote from the id and the gut.”

If you’re going to send a vampire rock star on tour, he’s going to need music. Series composer Hart, used to scoring the seasons after filming is complete, was tapped in summer 2024 to begin writing season three’s in-universe music performed by Reid, with Seamus Patterson, Sarah Swire, Ryan Kattner, and Noah Reid playing his band. Hart joined the writers’ room along with Kattner, who is also the lead singer and guitarist of experimental rock band Man Man. (Kattner plays Lestat’s bassist, Salamander, whom Lestat describes as “shockingly dumb” and who, while in character on set, told me with a shrug, “I’m just here to support my man, Lestat. He’s my prince.”) Jones and Hart worked out that Lestat wouldn’t be a nu-metal guy, as he had been in the film version of Queen of the Damned, but somewhere between David Bowie and Kurt Cobain, a mixture of the decadence of the 1970s and the stripped-down nature of early grunge. Hart wanted the music to feel like something an immortal would want to sing for decades, an idea inspired by Hart’s time opening for Bowie as part of the indie band the Polyphonic Spree in 2004 and watching Bowie perform 33-year-old songs each night. In the writers’ room, Hart was often writing two or three songs at a time and penned a couple dozen songs in total. (Hart and Kattner also have writing credits on season-three episodes.) The most exciting parts of season three’s writing process were “when Daniel would run in with a song,” Jones says.

Back in season one, Jones thought Lestat should be a symphony composer, imagining a setup where Lestat, like Jon Brion, would stand on a stage and invite an audience to yell out suggestions that he could build a song around. That approach would have required Reid to play more instruments than the violin and piano he’d already learned for songs he performed in previous seasons. The idea died when “somebody with a little bit more power than me at AMC sits me down and goes, ‘I don’t wanna see fucking vampire Tár,’” Jones says. By the middle of production on season two, Reid knew rock-star Lestat was his future. He started learning enough acoustic and electric guitar to credibly play parts of the songs onscreen and worked with vocal coach Matthew Santos to feel confident in the highest part of his vocal range and balance the demands on his voice, especially since Lestat is “screaming every second scene,” Reid laughs. “The songs started coming in, and they just got bigger and bigger and bigger and bigger. Now I have a less-eloquent, more emotionally driven lyricist figure and a very convoluted and intentionally twisty-turny way of speaking.” Reid listened to a playlist Hart sent him of about 70 songs from dozens of artists, including St. Vincent, Soundgarden, Chappell Roan, Heart, Depeche Mode, Black Sabbath, and Yeah Yeah Yeahs, to understand the influences shaping Lestat, and he researched references he didn’t immediately understand. “What the fuck is ‘uwu uwu Japanime eyes’?” Reid jokes about a line in his introduction to “Big Boss.”

However difficult the writing, rehearsals, and shooting may have been, it all made for a fascinating season of television, one in which Lestat is as loud and as quiet as he’s ever been, alternately explosive and pensive as he takes the stage, reconnects with Gabriella and Louis, and narrates his way through it all. For two seasons, this was Louis’s interview and Louis’s story, and Anderson performed that with a mixture of sophisticated precision and increasing ferality. The Vampire Lestat, meanwhile, is gonzo from the beginning. The season is deliriously frenetic, jam-packed with storylines, subplots, and concert sequences, and features more sex scenes and more outright gags and jokes. Episodes jerk around in time, flitter through different framing devices, and include versions of Louis, Armand, and Molloy who, now that they’re being presented by Lestat, don’t quite sound and act as they did when Louis was telling this story. The series is built to be rewatched, Jones says, and this season is the one he would personally return to; the words that keep coming up from its creators in our conversations include “dense” and “woven.”

The first minutes of premiere “Detroit” communicate the difference immediately, even before Reid’s winking, arch, agonized voiceover. IWTV had episodic title cards, but not a lengthy credits sequence. The Vampire Lestat does, with the single “All Fall Down” accompanying an opening montage of Lisa Frank neons, kaleidoscopic strobes, glittery graphics, and images including Lestat’s heart beating inside his chest and a halo around Lestat’s head as he floats onstage while playing his guitar. It’s a Baz Luhrmann–style feast, and it’s also “a little grottier and a little dirtier” than IWTV ever was, Jones says. A urinal full of bloody piss splashed away with Chardonnay? That’s so Lestat. “In previous seasons, he’ll come in and change a scene with his very intense energy, and he’ll leave everyone else in the wake of those — of his — repercussions,” Reid says. “But this season, it’s Lestat dealing with his own actions and his own choices.”

Lestat’s self-reflection speaks to one of the series’ driving thematic questions: What is the purpose of art, and who benefits — or suffers — most? The artist, the audience, the art itself? “Art is capricious. You can do art being like, ‘I’m in control of this,’ and it can get away from you,” Moscovitch says. “You open yourself up to do art, and you can heal yourself, and you can fuck yourself, and you can traumatize yourself or re-traumatize yourself. It’s not the safest of propositions to be like, I’m going to make art about my life if you haven’t thought much about it.’”

The Vampire Lestat filmed June through October 2025, and when I speak to Jones in early May 2026, post-production still isn’t quite done. He’s started working on AMC’s next big literary swing, the Great American Stories anthology that will begin with an adaptation of John Steinbeck’s transcendent The Grapes of Wrath, but he also has 17 visual-effects shots left to approve for this show. “There was a lot of storytelling done in post this year, and not in a ‘We gotta fix it’ way,” he says. “We found exploratory ways in which to tell the story.” If an episode seemed a little too slick or too engineered, Jones says, they had to mess it up to align it with Lestat’s at-odds psyche: keeping shots where the camera might have been bumped or frames that were slightly blurry if the performances felt right. “Every time it started to smell well made, it felt very false to me. That’s not what Lestat was going through, so you had to keep messing it up.”

What they have now is a season that Moscovitch says “more or less” adapts the entirety of Rice’s same-named novel, with an ending that could, if need be, serve as a series finale. Jones has “thoughts about a season four and a season five,” but the series hasn’t been renewed yet. Davis insists AMC remains committed to exploring further Rice adaptations despite the cancellation of Talamasca: The Secret Order, the third series in AMC’s Immortal Universe that premiered in October 2025. The series wasn’t directly based on a Rice book but centered on her in-universe secret society that monitors supernatural beings and whose representatives (like Justin Kirk’s Raglan James) have shown up in IWTV and The Mayfair Witches. Both Davis and Jones allude to other potential Vampire Chronicles series that could be developed with executive producer Mark Johnson. Ultimately, though, the season-four question is one for the cast and whether they have it “in their bodies,” Jones says. “There’s contracts and corporate desires and things like that, but if it was just me and Hannah and the actors, I would go, ‘You got any gas left in the tank?’”

The more immediate question is how viewers will react to The Vampire Lestat’s reinvention. The Heated Rivalry phenomena can’t be replicated, but could its fans find their way into the IWTV tent to fill the hole in their queer-romance hearts? Will established Loustat shippers vibe with a different protagonist, a different tone, and a different style? At least for the latter, early indications online seem promising, and there will be 1,000-plus fans attending a live performance by Reid in character as Lestat at the season’s June 2 premiere. (Hart pitched the spectacle during preproduction, and it has since inspired a countdown clock on one of the many sub-Reddits devoted to the series.) Hart and Reid have been preparing for the concert at New York’s Beacon Theatre for weeks, with rehearsals in Los Angeles and New York alongside a full band. Setlist details aren’t available yet, but Hart tells me about how a few crew members started to cry during the filming of the sixth episode, “Montreal,” when Reid was performing the ballad “Brutal Love.”

Whether or not viewers can make it to the premiere, Jones has a simple request for how they approach The Vampire Lestat overall: Trust him. “It will still be moving. It will still break your heart. It will still make you go crazy. It just has to do some bigger things,” he says. “It just ain’t two guys sitting in a room in Dubai.” Moscovitch believes in the fans. “They’re quite sophisticated,” she says. “And they’re pretty smutty. I’m smutty, too. You know how men like it when two women fuck? Guess what? That street, it actually goes that way and that way. Obviously women like watching two men fuck.”
